::: ltx_abstract
###### Abstract. {#abstract. .ltx_title .ltx_title_abstract}

Learning to optimize is a rapidly growing area that aims to solve
optimization problems or improve existing optimization algorithms using
machine learning (ML). In particular, the graph neural network (GNN) is
considered a suitable ML model for optimization problems whose variables
and constraints are permutation--invariant, for example, the linear
program (LP). While the literature has reported encouraging numerical
results, this paper establishes the theoretical foundation of applying
GNNs to solving LPs. Given any size limit of LPs, we construct a GNN
that maps different LPs to different outputs. We show that properly
built GNNs can reliably predict feasibility, boundedness, and an optimal
solution for each LP in a broad class. Our proofs are based upon the
recently--discovered connections between the Weisfeiler--Lehman
isomorphism test and the GNN. To validate our results, we train a simple
GNN and present its accuracy in mapping LPs to their feasibilities and
solutions.
:::

::: ltx_acknowledgements
A major part of the work of Z. Chen was completed during his internship
at Alibaba US DAMO Academy. Corresponding author: Jialin Liu,
jialin.liu@alibaba-inc.com
:::

::: {#S1 .section .ltx_section .ltx_indent_first}
## [1. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_section}Introduction {#introduction .ltx_title .ltx_title_section}

::: {#S1.p1 .ltx_para}
Applying machine learning (ML) techniques to accelerate optimization,
also known as *Learning to Optimize (L2O)*, is attracting increasing
attention recently. It has been reported in the literature that L2O
shows great potentials on both continuous
optimization \[[monga2021algorithm]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [chen2021learning]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [amos2022tutorial]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\] and combinatorial optimization
problems \[[bengio2021machine]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [mazyavkina2021reinforcement]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\] Many of the L2O works train a
parameterized model that takes the optimization problem as input and
outputs information useful to the classic algorithms, such as a good
initial solution and branching decisions \[[nair2020solving]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\], and some even directly generate
an approximate optimal solution \[[gregor2010learning]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\].
:::

::: {#S1.p2 .ltx_para}
In these works, one is building an ML model to approximate the mapping
from an explicit optimization instance either to its key properties or
directly to its solution. The accuracy of this approximation is called
the representation power or expressive power of the model. When the
approximation is accurate, the model can solve the problem or provide
useful information to guide an optimization algorithm. This paper tries
to address a fundamental but open theoretical problem for linear
programming (LP):

  --------------------------------------------------------- -- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(P0)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equationgroup .ltx_align_left}         [Which neural network can represent LP and predict its key properties and solution?]{#S1.Ex1X.2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_markedasmath .ltx_font_italic}   
  --------------------------------------------------------- -- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

To clarify, by solution we mean the optimal solution. We hope the answer
to this question paves the way toward answering this question for other
optimization types.
:::

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px1 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Linear Programming (LP) {#linear-programming-lp .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p1 .ltx_para}
LP is an important type of optimization problem with a long history and
a wide range of applications, such as
scheduling \[[hanssmann1960linear]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\], signal processing \[[candes2005decoding]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\], machine
learning \[[dedieu2022solving]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\], etc. A general LP problem is defined as:

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(1.1)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $$\begin{array}{r}                                                                                                                                
                                                           {{{{\min\limits_{x \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}}{c^{\top}\operatorname{}x}},{{\text{s.t.}\operatorname{}A\operatorname{}x} \circ b},l} \leq x \leq u},}   
                                                           \end{array}$$                                                                                                                                     
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

where $A \in {\mathbb{R}}^{m \times n}$, $c \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}$,
$b \in {\mathbb{R}}^{m}$,
$l \in {({{\mathbb{R}} \cup {\{{- \infty}\}}})}^{n}$,
$u \in {({{\mathbb{R}} \cup {\{{+ \infty}\}}})}^{n}$, and
$\circ \in {\{ \leq , = , \geq \}}^{m}$. Any LP problems must follow one
of the following three cases \[[bertsimas1997introduction]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\]:

-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I1.i1}

    ::: {#S1.I1.i1.p1 .ltx_para}
    Infeasible. The feasible set
    $\mathcal{X}_{F}:={\{{x \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}}:{{{{A\operatorname{}x} \circ b},l} \leq x \leq u}\}}$
    is empty. In another word, there is no point in ${\mathbb{R}}^{n}$
    that satisfies the constraints in LP ([[1.1]{.ltx_text
    .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.E1 "1.1 ‣ Linear Programming (LP) ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}).
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I1.i2}

    ::: {#S1.I1.i2.p1 .ltx_para}
    Unbounded. The feasible set is non-empty, but the objective value
    can be arbitrarily good, i.e., lower unbounded. For any $R > 0$,
    there exists an $x \in \mathcal{X}_{F}$ such that
    ${c^{\top}\operatorname{}x} < {- R}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I1.i3}

    ::: {#S1.I1.i3.p1 .ltx_para}
    Feasible and bounded. There exists $x^{\ast} \in \mathcal{X}_{F}$
    such that
    ${c^{\top}\operatorname{}x^{\ast}} \leq {c^{\top}\operatorname{}x}$
    for all $x \in \mathcal{X}_{F}$. Such $x^{\ast}$ is named as an
    optimal solution, and $c^{\top}\operatorname{}x^{\ast}$ is the
    optimal objective value.
    :::
:::

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p2 .ltx_para}
Thus, considering ([[P0]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex1 "P0 ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
on the LP problem ([[1.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.E1 "1.1 ‣ Linear Programming (LP) ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),
an ideal ML model is expected to be able to predict the three key
characteristics of LP: *feasibility, boundedness, and one of its optimal
solutions* (if exist), by taking the LP features $(A,b,c,l,u, \circ )$
as input.
:::

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p3 .ltx_para}
Such input has strong mathematical structure. If we swap the position of
the $i,j$-th variable in ([[1.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.E1 "1.1 ‣ Linear Programming (LP) ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),
elements in vectors $b,c,l,u, \circ$ and columns of matrix $A$ will be
reordered. The reordered features
$(\hat{A},b,\hat{c},\hat{l},\hat{u},\hat{\circ})$ actually represent an
exact equivalent LP problem with the original one $(A,b,c,l,u, \circ )$.
Such property is named as *permutation invariance*. If we do not
explicitly restrict ML models with a permutation invariant structure,
the models may overfit the variable/constraint orders of instances in
the training set. Motivated by this point, we adopt *Graph Neural
Networks (GNNs)* that are permutation invariant naturally.
:::
:::

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px2 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### GNN in L2O {#gnn-in-l2o .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px2.p1 .ltx_para}
GNN is a type of neural network defined on graphs and widely applied in
many areas, for example, recommender systems, traffic, chemistry, etc
\[[wu2020comprehensive]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
[zhou2020graph]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\]. Solving
optimization problems with GNN is a recent arising topic in the
literature \[[peng2021graph]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [ijcai2021-595]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. Many graph-related optimization problems, like minimum
vertex cover, traveling salesman problem, vehicle routing problem, can
be represented and solved approximately with GNN due to their problem
structure \[[khalil2017learning]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [kool2018attention]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [joshi2019efficient]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [drori2020learning]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. Besides that, one may solve a general LP or
mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) with the help of GNN.
\[[gasse2019exact]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\]
proposed to represent an MILP problem with a bipartite graph, then
applied a message-passing GNN[^1^[[^1^[1]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_note}We will
abbreviate message-passing GNN as GNN in this paper for
simplicity.]{.ltx_note_content}]{.ltx_note_outer}]{#footnote1 .ltx_note
.ltx_role_footnote} on this graph to guide a classic MILP solver. Since
that, many approaches have been proposed to guide MILP or LP solvers
with GNN \[[nair2020solving]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [gupta2020hybrid]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [ding2020accelerating]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [shen2021learning]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [gupta2022lookback]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [khalil2022mip]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [liu2022learning]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [paulus2022learning]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [qu2022improved]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [li2022learning]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. Although encouraging empirical results have been
observed, theoretical foundations are still lack for this approach.
Specifying ([[P0]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex1 "P0 ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),
we ask:

  --------------------------------------------------------- -- -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(P1)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equationgroup .ltx_align_left}         [Are there GNNs that can predict the feasibility, boundedness,]{#S1.Ex2X.2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_markedasmath .ltx_font_italic}   
                                                                  [and an optimal solution (if exists), for all LPs?]{#S1.Ex2Xa.2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_markedasmath .ltx_font_italic}              
  --------------------------------------------------------- -- -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
:::
:::

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px3 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Related works and contributions {#related-works-and-contributions .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px3.p1 .ltx_para}
To answer ([[P1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex2 "P1 ‣ GNN in L2O ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),
one needs the theory of *separation power* and *representation power*.
Separation power of a neural network (NN) means its ability to
distinguish two different inputs. In our settings, a NN with strong
separation power means that it can outputs different results when it is
applied on any two different LPs. Representation power of NN means its
ability to approximate functions of interest. A famous example of
representation power is the Universal Approximation Theorem
\[[hornik1989multilayer]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
[cybenko1989approximation]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]: multilayer perceptron (MLP), a specific type of NN,
can universally approximate continuous functions on a compact domain.
The theory of representation power is established upon the theory of
separation power. Only functions with strong enough separation power may
possess strong representation power. The power of GNN has been studied
in the literature (see \[[sato2020survey]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [jegelka2022theory]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [Li2022]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\]
for comprehensive surveys), and some theoretical efforts have been made
to represent some graph-related optimization problems with
GNN \[[sato2019approximation]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [Loukas2020What]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. However, there are still gaps to answer question
([[P1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex2 "P1 ‣ GNN in L2O ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
since the relationships between characteristics of LP and properties of
graphs are not well established. Our contributions are listed below:

-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I2.i1}

    ::: {#S1.I2.i1.p1 .ltx_para}
    (Separation Power). In the literature, it has been shown that the
    separation power of GNN is equal to the WL
    test\[[xu2019powerful]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
    .ltx_ref_self}, [azizian2020expressive]{.ltx_ref
    .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
    [geerts2022expressiveness]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
    .ltx_ref_self}\]. However, there exist many pairs of LPs that cannot
    be distinguished by the WL test. We show that those puzzling LP
    pairs share the same feasibility, boundedness, and even an optimal
    solution if exists. Thus, GNN has strong enough separation power.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I2.i2}

    ::: {#S1.I2.i2.p1 .ltx_para}
    (Representation Power). To the best of our knowledge, we established
    the first complete proof that GNN can universally represent a broad
    class of LPs. More precisely, we prove that, there exist GNNs that
    can be arbitrarily close to the following three mappings:

    -   [[[--]{#S1.I2.i2.I1.i1.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag
        .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I2.i2.I1.i1}

        ::: {#S1.I2.i2.I1.i1.p1 .ltx_para}
        LP $\rightarrow$ feasibility,
        :::
    -   [[[--]{#S1.I2.i2.I1.i2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag
        .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I2.i2.I1.i2}

        ::: {#S1.I2.i2.I1.i2.p1 .ltx_para}
        LP $\rightarrow$ optimal objective value ($- \infty$ if
        unbounded and $\infty$ if infeasible),
        :::
    -   [[[--]{#S1.I2.i2.I1.i3.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag
        .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I2.i2.I1.i3}

        ::: {#S1.I2.i2.I1.i3.p1 .ltx_para}
        LP $\rightarrow$ an optimal solution (if exists),
        :::

    although they are not continuous functions and cannot be covered by
    the literature\[[keriven2019universal]{.ltx_ref
    .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}, [chen2019equivalence]{.ltx_ref
    .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}, [maron2019provably]{.ltx_ref
    .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
    [maron2019universality]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
    .ltx_ref_self}, [keriven2021universality]{.ltx_ref
    .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\].
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S1.I2.i3}

    ::: {#S1.I2.i3.p1 .ltx_para}
    (Experimental Validation). We design and conduct experiments that
    demonstrate the power of GNN on representing LP.
    :::
:::

::: {#S1.SS0.SSS0.Px3.p2 .ltx_para}
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. In Section
[[2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2 "2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
we provide preliminaries, including related notions, definitions and
concepts. In Section [[3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3 "3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
we show our main theoretical results. The sketches of proofs are
provided in Section[[4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4 "4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
We validate our results with numerical experiments in Section
[[5]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5 "5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
and we conclude this paper with Section [[6]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S6 "6. Conclusions ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
:::
:::
:::

::: {#S2 .section .ltx_section .ltx_indent_first}
## [2. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_section}Preliminaries {#preliminaries .ltx_title .ltx_title_section}

::: {#S2.p1 .ltx_para}
In this section, we present concepts and definitions that will be used
throughout this paper. We first describe how to represent an LP with a
weighted bipartite graph, then we define GNN on those LP-induced graphs,
and finally we further clarify question ([[P1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex2 "P1 ‣ GNN in L2O ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
with strict mathematical definitions.
:::

::: {#S2.SS1 .section .ltx_subsection .ltx_indent_first}
### [2.1. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_subsection}LP represented as weighted bipartite graph {#lp-represented-as-weighted-bipartite-graph .ltx_title .ltx_title_subsection}

::: {#S2.SS1.p1 .ltx_para}
Before representing LPs with graphs, we first define the graph that we
will adopt in this paper: *weighted bipartite graph*. A weighted
bipartite graph $G = {({V \cup W},E)}$ consists of a vertex set
$V \cup W$ that are divided into two groups $V$ and $W$ with
${V \cap W} = \varnothing$, and a collection $E$ of weighted edges,
where each edge connects exactly one vertex in $V$ and one vertex in
$W$. Note that there is no edge connecting vertices in the same vertex
group. $E$ can also be viewed as a function
$E:{{V \times W}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}}$. We use $\mathcal{G}_{m,n}$ to
denote the collection of all weighted bipartite graphs
$G = {({V \cup W},E)}$ with ${|V|} = m$ and ${|W|} = n$. We always write
$V = {\{ v_{1},v_{2},\ldots,v_{m}\}}$,
$W = {\{ w_{1},w_{2},\ldots,w_{n}\}}$, and
$E_{i,j} = {E\operatorname{}{(v_{i},w_{j})}}$, for
${i \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,m\}}},{j \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}}}$.
:::

::: {#S2.SS1.p2 .ltx_para}
One can equip each vertex with a feature vector. Throughout this paper,
we denote $h_{i}^{V} \in \mathcal{H}^{V}$ as the feature vector of
vertex $v_{i} \in V$ and denote $h_{j}^{W} \in \mathcal{H}^{W}$ as the
feature vector of vertex $w_{j} \in W$, where
$\mathcal{H}^{V},\mathcal{H}^{W}$ are feature spaces. Then we define
${\mathcal{H}_{m}^{V}:={(\mathcal{H}^{V})}^{m}},{\mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}:={(\mathcal{H}^{W})}^{n}}$
and concatenate all the vertex features together as
$H = {(h_{1}^{V},h_{2}^{V},\ldots,h_{m}^{V},h_{1}^{W},h_{2}^{W},\ldots,h_{n}^{W})} \in {\mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$.
Finally, a weighted bipartite graph with vertex features is defined as a
tuple
${(G,H)} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$.
:::

::: {#S2.SS1.p3 .ltx_para}
With the concepts described above, one can represent an LP
([[1.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.E1 "1.1 ‣ Linear Programming (LP) ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
as a graph \[[gasse2019exact]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. Each vertex in $W$ represents a variable in LP and
each vertex in $V$ represents a constraint. The graph topology is
defined with the matrix $A$ in the linear constraint. More specifically,
let us set

-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I1.i1}

    ::: {#S2.I1.i1.p1 .ltx_para}
    Vertex $v_{i}$ represents the $i$-th constraint in
    ${A\operatorname{}x} \circ b$, and vertex $w_{j}$ represents the
    $j$-th variable $x_{j}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I1.i2}

    ::: {#S2.I1.i2.p1 .ltx_para}
    Information of constraints is involved in the feature of $v_{i}$:
    $h_{i}^{V} = {(b_{i}, \circ_{i})}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I1.i3}

    ::: {#S2.I1.i3.p1 .ltx_para}
    The space of constraint features is defined as
    $\mathcal{H}^{V}:={{\mathbb{R}} \times {\{ \leq , = , \geq \}}}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I1.i4}

    ::: {#S2.I1.i4.p1 .ltx_para}
    Information of variables is involved in the feature of $w_{i}$:
    $h_{j}^{W} = {(c_{j},l_{j},u_{j})}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I1.i5}

    ::: {#S2.I1.i5.p1 .ltx_para}
    The space of variable features is defined as
    $\mathcal{H}^{W}:={{\mathbb{R}} \times {({{\mathbb{R}} \cup {\{{- \infty}\}}})} \times {({{\mathbb{R}} \cup {\{{+ \infty}\}}})}}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I1.i6}

    ::: {#S2.I1.i6.p1 .ltx_para}
    The edge connecting $v_{i}$ and $w_{j}$ has weight
    $E_{i,j} = A_{i,j}$.
    :::

Then an LP is represented as a graph
${(G,H)} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$.
In the rest of this paper, we coin such graphs as *LP-induced Graphs* or
*LP-Graphs* for simplicity. We present an LP instance and its
corresponding LP-graph in Figure [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 2.1. LP represented as weighted bipartite graph ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
:::

::: {#S2.SS2 .section .ltx_subsection .ltx_indent_first}
### [2.2. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_subsection}Graph neural networks for LP {#graph-neural-networks-for-lp .ltx_title .ltx_title_subsection}

::: {#S2.SS2.p1 .ltx_para}
Now we define GNNs on LP-graphs. The GNNs in this paper always take an
LP-Graph as input and the output has two cases:

-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I2.i1}

    ::: {#S2.I2.i1.p1 .ltx_para}
    The output is a single real number. In this case, GNN is a function
    ${\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}$
    and usually used to predict the properties of the whole graph.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S2.I2.i2}

    ::: {#S2.I2.i2.p1 .ltx_para}
    Each vertex in $W$ has an output. Consequently, GNN is a function
    ${\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{n}$.
    Since $W$ represents variables in LP, GNN is used to predict
    properties of each variable in this case.
    :::

Now we define the GNN structure precisely. First we encode the input
features into the embedding space with learnable functions
$f_{in}^{V}:{\mathcal{H}^{V}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{0}}}$ and
$f_{in}^{W}:{\mathcal{H}^{W}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{0}}}$

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
  [(2.1)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${{h_{i}^{0,V} = {f_{in}^{V}\operatorname{}{(h_{i}^{V})}}},{{h_{j}^{0,W} = {f_{in}^{W}\operatorname{}{(h_{j}^{W})}}},{{i = {1,2,\ldots,m}},{j = {1,2,\ldots,n}}}}},$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

where ${h_{i}^{0,V},h_{j}^{0,W}} \in {\mathbb{R}}^{d_{0}}$ are initial
embedded vertex features, $d_{0}$ is their dimension. Then we choose
learnable functions
${f_{l}^{V},f_{l}^{W}}:{{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{l - 1}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{l}}}$
and
${g_{l}^{V},g_{l}^{W}}:{{{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{l - 1}} \times {\mathbb{R}}^{d_{l}}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{l}}}$
and update the hidden states with[^2^[[^2^[2]{.ltx_tag
.ltx_tag_note}Note that the update rules in ([[2.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E2 "2.2 ‣ 2.2. Graph neural networks for LP ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
and ([[2.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E3 "2.3 ‣ 2.2. Graph neural networks for LP ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
follow a message-passing way, where each vertex only collects
information from its neighbors. Since $E_{i,j} = 0$ if there is no
connection between vertices $v_{i}$ and $w_{j}$, the sum operator in
([[2.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E2 "2.2 ‣ 2.2. Graph neural networks for LP ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
can be rewritten as
$\sum_{j \in {\mathcal{N}\operatorname{}{(v_{i})}}}$, where
$\mathcal{N}\operatorname{}{(v_{i})}$ denotes the set of neighbors of
vertex $v_{i}$.]{.ltx_note_content}]{.ltx_note_outer}]{#footnote2
.ltx_note .ltx_role_footnote}:

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(2.2)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      ${{h_{i}^{l,V} = {g_{l}^{V}\operatorname{}\left( h_{i}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}{(h_{j}^{{l - 1},W})}}} \right)}},{i = {1,2,\ldots,m}}},$   
  [(2.3)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      ${{h_{j}^{l,W} = {g_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}\left( h_{j}^{{l - 1},W},{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{m}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{V}\operatorname{}{(h_{i}^{{l - 1},V})}}} \right)}},{j = {1,2,\ldots,n}}},$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

where ${h_{i}^{l,V},h_{j}^{l,W}} \in {\mathbb{R}}^{d_{l}}$ are vertex
features are layer $l\operatorname{}{({1 \leq l \leq L})}$ and their
dimensions are $d_{1},\cdots,d_{L}$ respectively. The output layer of
the single-output GNN is defined with learnable function
$f_{\text{out}}:{{{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{L}} \times {\mathbb{R}}^{d_{L}}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}}$

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
  [(2.4)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${y_{out} = {f_{\text{out}}\operatorname{}\left( {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{m}h_{i}^{L,V}},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}h_{j}^{L,W}} \right)}}.$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

The output of the vertex-output GNN is defined with learnable function
$f_{\text{out}}^{W}:{{{\mathbb{R}}^{d_{L}} \times {\mathbb{R}}^{d_{L}} \times {\mathbb{R}}^{d_{L}}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}}$

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(2.5)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${{{y_{out}\operatorname{}{(w_{j})}} = {f_{\text{out}}^{W}\operatorname{}\left( {\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{m}h_{i}^{L,V}},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}h_{j}^{L,W}},h_{j}^{L,W} \right)}},{j = {1,2,\cdots,n}}}.$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

We denote collections of single-output GNNs and vertex-output GNNs with
$\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}$ and $\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}^{W}$
respectively.

  ---------------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(2.6)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equationgroup .ltx_align_left}      $\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}} =$       ${\{ F:{{\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow\left. {\mathbb{R}} \middle| {{F\operatorname{}\text{~yields~}\operatorname{}(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})},{(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})},{(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})},{(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})}} \right.}\}},$       
                                                                $\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}^{W} =$   ${\{ F:{{\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow\left. {\mathbb{R}}^{n} \middle| {{F\operatorname{}\text{~yields~}\operatorname{}(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})},{(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})},{(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})},{(\operatorname{}\operatorname{})}} \right.}\}}.$   
  ---------------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

In practice, all the learnable functions in GNN
$f_{in}^{V},f_{in}^{W},f_{\text{out}},f_{\text{out}}^{W},{\{ f_{l}^{V},f_{l}^{W},g_{l}^{V},g_{l}^{W}\}}_{l = 0}^{L}$
are usually parameterized with multi-linear perceptrons (MLP). In our
theoretical analysis, we assume for simplicity that those functions may
take all continuous functions on given domains, following the settings
in \[[azizian2020expressive]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]\*Section C.1. Thanks to the universal approximation
properties of MLP \[[hornik1989multilayer]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
[cybenko1989approximation]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\], one can extend our theoretical results by taking those
learnable functions as large enough MLPs.
:::
:::

::: {#S2.SS3 .section .ltx_subsection .ltx_indent_first}
### [2.3. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_subsection}Revisiting question ([[P1]{.ltx_text .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex2 "P1 ‣ GNN in L2O ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}) {#revisiting-question-p1 .ltx_title .ltx_title_subsection}

::: {#S2.SS3.p1 .ltx_para}
With the definitions of LP-Graph and GNN defined above, we revisit
question ([[P1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex2 "P1 ‣ GNN in L2O ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
and give a more precise mathematical description here. First we define
three mappings that respectively describe the feasibility, optimal
objective value and an optimal solution of an LP.
:::

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px1 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Feasibility mapping {#feasibility-mapping .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px1.p1 .ltx_para}
The feasibility mapping is a classification function

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(2.7)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${\Phi_{\text{feas}}:{{\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow{\{ 0,1\}}}},$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

where ${\Phi_{\text{feas}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = 1$ if the LP
associated with $(G,H)$ is feasible and
${\Phi_{\text{feas}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = 0$ otherwise.
:::
:::

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px2 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Optimal objective value mapping {#optimal-objective-value-mapping .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px2.p1 .ltx_para}
Denote

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
  [(2.8)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${\Phi_{\text{obj}}:{{\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow{{\mathbb{R}} \cup {\{\infty,{- \infty}\}}}}},$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

as the optimal objective value mapping, i.e., for any
${(G,H)} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$,
$\Phi_{\text{obj}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}$ is the optimal objective value
of the LP problem associated with $(G,H)$.
:::

::: {#S2.Thmtheorem1 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_remark}
###### [[Remark 2.1]{#S2.Thmtheorem1.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S2.Thmtheorem1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#remark-2.1. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S2.Thmtheorem1.p1 .ltx_para}
[The optimal objective value of a LP problem can be a real number or
$\infty/ - \infty$. The "$\infty$" case corresponds to infeasible
problems, while the "$- \infty$" case consists of LP problems whose
objective function is unbounded from below in the feasible region. The
preimage of all finite real numbers under $\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}$,
$\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}$, actually
describes all LPs with finite optimal objective
value.]{#S2.Thmtheorem1.p1.5.5 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#S2.Thmtheorem2 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_remark}
###### [[Remark 2.2]{#S2.Thmtheorem2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S2.Thmtheorem2.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#remark-2.2. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S2.Thmtheorem2.p1 .ltx_para}
[In the case that a LP problem has finite optimal objective value, it is
possible that the problem admits multiple optimal solutions. However,
the optimal solution with the smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm must be unique.
In fact, if $x \neq x^{\prime}$ are two different solutions with
${\| x\|} = {\| x^{\prime}\|}$. Then
$\frac{1}{2}\operatorname{}{({x + x^{\prime}})}$ is also an optimal
solution due to the convexity of LPs, and it holds that
${\|{\frac{1}{2}\operatorname{}{({x + x^{\prime}})}}\|}^{2} < {{\frac{1}{2}\operatorname{}{\| x\|}^{2}} + {\frac{1}{2}\operatorname{}{\| x^{\prime}\|}^{2}}} = {\| x\|}^{2} = {\| x^{\prime}\|}^{2}$,
i.e.,
${\|{\frac{1}{2}\operatorname{}{({x + x^{\prime}})}}\|} < {\| x\|} = {\| x^{\prime}\|}$,
where the inequality is strict since $x \neq x^{\prime}$. Therefore, $x$
and $x^{\prime}$ cannot be optimal solutions with the smallest
$\ell_{2}$-norm.]{#S2.Thmtheorem2.p1.10.10 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::
:::

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px3 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Optimal solution mapping {#optimal-solution-mapping .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px3.p1 .ltx_para}
For any
${(G,H)} \in {\Phi_{\text{obj}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}}$,
we have remarked before that the LP problem associated with $(G,H)$ has
a unique optimal solution with the smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm. Let

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(2.9)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${\Phi_{\text{solu}}:{{\Phi_{\text{obj}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{n}}},$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

be the mapping that maps
${(G,H)} \in {\Phi_{\text{obj}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}}$
to the optimal solution with the smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm.
:::
:::

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px4 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Invariance and Equivariance {#invariance-and-equivariance .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px4.p1 .ltx_para}
We denote $S_{m},S_{n}$ as the group consisting of all permutations on
vertex groups $V,W$ respectively. In another word, $S_{m}$ involves all
permutations on the constraints of LP and $S_{n}$ involves all
permutations on the variables. In this paper, we say a function
$F:{{\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}}$
is invariant if it satisfies

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {F\operatorname{}\left( {{(\sigma_{V},\sigma_{W})} \ast {(G,H)}} \right)}},{{{\forall\sigma_{V}} \in S_{m}},{\sigma_{W} \in S_{n}}}},$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

and a function
$F_{W}:{{\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{n}}$
is equivariant if it satisfies

  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $$\left. \sigma_{W}{(F_{W}{(G,H)})} = F_{W}\left( {(\sigma_{V},\sigma_{W})} \ast {(G,H)}) \right. \right),\forall\sigma_{V} \in S_{m},\sigma_{W} \in S_{n},$$   
  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

where ${(\sigma_{V},\sigma_{W})} \ast {(G,H)}$ is the permuted graph
obtained from reordering indices in $(G,H)$ using
$(\sigma_{V},\sigma_{W})$, which is the group action
$S_{m} \times S_{n}$ on
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}$.
One can check that $\Phi_{\text{feas}}$ and $\Phi_{\text{obj}}$ and any
$F \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}$ are invariant, $\Phi_{\text{solu}}$ and
any $F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}^{W}$ are equivariant.
:::

::: {#S2.SS3.SSS0.Px4.p2 .ltx_para}
Question ([[P1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex2 "P1 ‣ GNN in L2O ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
actually asks: are there GNNs that can accurately approximate
$\Phi_{\text{feas}}$, $\Phi_{\text{obj}}$ and $\Phi_{\text{solu}}$? More
precisely, does there exist $F \in \mathcal{F}_{GNN}$ that is
arbitrarily close to $\Phi_{\text{feas}}$ and $\Phi_{\text{obj}}$? And
does there exist $F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{GNN}^{W}$ that is arbitrarily
close to $\Phi_{\text{solu}}$?
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {#S3 .section .ltx_section .ltx_indent_first}
## [3. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_section}Main results {#main-results .ltx_title .ltx_title_section}

::: {#S3.p1 .ltx_para}
This section presents our main theorems that answer question
([[P1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S1.Ex2 "P1 ‣ GNN in L2O ‣ 1. Introduction ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}).
As we state in the introduction, representation power is built upon
separation power in our paper. We first present with the following
theorem that GNN has strong enough separation power to represent LP.
:::

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem1 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem 3.1]{#S3.Thmtheorem1.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S3.Thmtheorem1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-3.1. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem1.p1 .ltx_para}
[For any two LP problems respectively corresponding to
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$,
if
${{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {F\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}},{{\forall F} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}}$,
then they share some common characteristics:]{#S3.Thmtheorem1.p1.2.2
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

-   [[(i)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S3.I1.ix1}

    ::: {#S3.I1.ix1.p1 .ltx_para}
    [Both LP problems are feasible or both are infeasible, i.e.,
    ]{#S3.I1.ix1.p1.1.1 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}${\Phi_{\text{𝑓𝑒𝑎𝑠}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\Phi_{\text{𝑓𝑒𝑎𝑠}}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}$[.]{#S3.I1.ix1.p1.1.2
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
-   [[(ii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S3.I1.ix2}

    ::: {#S3.I1.ix2.p1 .ltx_para}
    [The two LP problems have the same optimal objective value, i.e.,
    ]{#S3.I1.ix2.p1.1.1 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}${\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}$[.]{#S3.I1.ix2.p1.1.2
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
-   [[(iii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S3.I1.ix3}

    ::: {#S3.I1.ix3.p1 .ltx_para}
    [If both problems are feasible with finite optimal objective value,
    then they have the same optimal solution with the smallest
    ]{#S3.I1.ix3.p1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}$\ell_{2}$[-norm, up
    to a permutation.]{#S3.I1.ix3.p1.1.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::

[Furthermore, if
${{F_{W}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {F_{W}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}},{{\forall F_{W}} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}^{W}}$,
then (iii) holds without taking permutations, i.e.,
${\Phi_{\text{𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑢}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\Phi_{\text{𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑢}}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}$.]{#S3.Thmtheorem1.p1.4.2
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#S3.p2 .ltx_para}
This theorem demonstrates that the function spaces $\mathcal{F}_{GNN}$
and $\mathcal{F}_{GNN}^{W}$ are rich enough to distinguish the
characteristics of LP. Given two LP instances
${(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$, as long as their feasibility or
boundedness are different, there must exist $F \in \mathcal{F}_{GNN}$
that can distinguish them:
${F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} \neq {F\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}$.
Moreover, as long as their optimal solutions with the smallest
$\ell_{2}$-norm are different, there must exist
$F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{GNN}^{W}$ that can distinguish them:
${F_{W}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} \neq {F_{W}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}$.
:::

::: {#S3.p3 .ltx_para}
With Theorem [[3.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem1 "Theorem 3.1. ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
served as a foundation, we can prove that GNN universally approximates
the three mappings $\Phi_{\text{feas}}$, $\Phi_{\text{obj}}$ and
$\Phi_{\text{solu}}$. Before presenting those results, we first define
some concepts of the space
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}$.
:::

::: {#S3.SS0.SSS0.Px1 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Topology and measure {#topology-and-measure .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S3.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p1 .ltx_para}
Let us define the topology and the measure on
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}$,
where $\mathcal{H}^{V} = {{\mathbb{R}} \times {\{ \leq , = , \geq \}}}$
and
$\mathcal{H}^{W} = {{\mathbb{R}} \times {({{\mathbb{R}} \cup {\{{- \infty}\}}})} \times {({{\mathbb{R}} \cup {\{{+ \infty}\}}})}}$.
Throughout this paper, we consider
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}$
as a topology space with product topology and measurable space with
product measure. It's enough to define the topology and measure of each
part separately. Since each graph in this paper (without vertex
features) can be represented with matrix
$A \in {\mathbb{R}}^{m \times n}$, the graph space $\mathcal{G}_{m,n}$
is isomorphic to the Euclidean space ${\mathbb{R}}^{m \times n}$:
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \cong {\mathbb{R}}^{m \times n}$ and we equip
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n}$ with the standard Euclidean topology and the
standard Lebesgue measure. The real spaces $\mathbb{R}$ in
$\mathcal{H}^{W}$ and $\mathcal{H}^{V}$ are also equipped with the
standard Euclidean topology and Lebesgue measure. All the discrete
spaces $\{ \leq , = , \geq \}$, $\{{- \infty}\}$, and $\{{+ \infty}\}$
have the discrete topology, and all unions are disjoint unions. We equip
those spaces with a discrete measure
${\mu\operatorname{}{(S)}} = {|S|}$, where $|S|$ is the number of
elements in a discrete space $S$. This finishes the whole definition and
we denote $\text{Meas}\operatorname{}{( \cdot )}$ as the measure on
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}$.
:::

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem2 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem 3.2]{#S3.Thmtheorem2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S3.Thmtheorem2.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-3.2. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem2.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
$X \subset {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
be measurable with finite measure. For any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists
some $F \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}$, such that]{#S3.Thmtheorem2.p1.3.3
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\text{𝑀𝑒𝑎𝑠}\operatorname{}\left( \left\{ {{(G,H)} \in X}:{{\mathbb{I}}_{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}} \neq {\Phi_{\text{𝑓𝑒𝑎𝑠}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}} \right\} \right)} < \epsilon},$$   
  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

[where ${\mathbb{I}}_{\cdot}$ is the indicator function, i.e.,
${\mathbb{I}}_{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}} = 1$ if
${F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}$ and
${\mathbb{I}}_{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}} = 0$
otherwise.]{#S3.Thmtheorem2.p1.7.4 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#S3.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p2 .ltx_para}
This theorem shows that GNN is a good classifier for LP instances in $X$
as long as $X$ has finite measure. If we use
${F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}$ as the criteria to predict the
feasibility, the classification error rate is controlled by
${\epsilon/\text{Meas}}\operatorname{}{(X)}$, where $\epsilon$ can be
arbitrarily small. Furthermore, we show that GNN can perfectly fit any
dataset with finite samples, which is presented in the following
corollary.
:::

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem3 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_corollary}
###### [[Corollary 3.3]{#S3.Thmtheorem3.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S3.Thmtheorem3.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#corollary-3.3. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem3.p1 .ltx_para}
[For any
$\mathcal{D} \subset {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
with finite instances, there exists $F \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}$
that]{#S3.Thmtheorem3.p1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{{\mathbb{I}}_{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}} = {\Phi_{\text{𝑓𝑒𝑎𝑠}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}},{{\forall{(G,H)}} \in \mathcal{D}}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
:::
:::

::: {#S3.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p3 .ltx_para}
Besides feasibility, GNN can also approximate the optimal objective
value and the optimal solution with the smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm up to
any given precision. The following theorems provide formal statements.
:::

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem4 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem 3.4]{#S3.Thmtheorem4.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S3.Thmtheorem4.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-3.4. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem4.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
$X \subset {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
be measurable with finite measure. For any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists
$F_{1} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}$ such that]{#S3.Thmtheorem4.p1.3.3
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(3.1)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${{\text{𝑀𝑒𝑎𝑠}\operatorname{}\left( \left\{ {{(G,H)} \in X}:{{\mathbb{I}}_{{F_{1}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}} \neq {\mathbb{I}}_{{\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} \in {\mathbb{R}}}} \right\} \right)} < \epsilon}.$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

[For any ${\epsilon,\delta} > 0$, there exists
$F_{2} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}$ such that]{#S3.Thmtheorem4.p1.5.2
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(3.2)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${{\text{𝑀𝑒𝑎𝑠}\operatorname{}\left( \left\{ {{(G,H)} \in {X \cap {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}}}}:{{|{{F_{2}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} - {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}}|} > \delta} \right\} \right)} < \epsilon}.$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
:::
:::

::: {#S3.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p4 .ltx_para}
Recall the definition of $\Phi_{\text{obj}}$ in ([[2.8]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E8 "2.8 ‣ Optimal objective value mapping ‣ 2.3. Revisiting question (P1) ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
that it can take $\{{\pm \infty}\}$ as its value. Thus,
${\Phi_{\text{obj}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} \in {\mathbb{R}}$ means the
LP corresponding to $(G,H)$ is feasible and bounded with a finite
optimal objective value, and inequality ([[3.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.E1 "3.1 ‣ Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
illustrates that GNN can identify those feasible and bounded LPs among
the whole set $X$, up to a given precision $\epsilon$. Inequality
([[3.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.E2 "3.2 ‣ Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
shows that GNN can also approximate the optimal value. The measure of
the set of LP instances of which the optimal value cannot be
approximated with $\delta$-precision is controlled by $\epsilon$. The
following corollary gives the results on dataset with finite instances.
:::

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem5 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_corollary}
###### [[Corollary 3.5]{#S3.Thmtheorem5.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S3.Thmtheorem5.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#corollary-3.5. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem5.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
$\mathcal{D} \subset {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
be a finite dataset. There exists $F_{1} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}$
such that]{#S3.Thmtheorem5.p1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
     $${{{\mathbb{I}}_{{F_{1}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}} = {\mathbb{I}}_{{\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} \in {\mathbb{R}}}},{{\forall{(G,H)}} \in \mathcal{D}}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

[For any $\delta > 0$, there exists
$F_{2} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}$, such that]{#S3.Thmtheorem5.p1.4.2
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
     $${{{|{{F_{2}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} - {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}}|} < \delta},{{\forall{(G,H)}} \in {\mathcal{D} \cap {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}}}}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
:::
:::

::: {#S3.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p5 .ltx_para}
Finally, we show that GNN is able to represent the solution mapping
$\Phi_{\text{solu}}$.
:::

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem6 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem 3.6]{#S3.Thmtheorem6.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S3.Thmtheorem6.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-3.6. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem6.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
$X \subset {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}} \subset {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
be measurable with finite measure and be closed under actions in
$S_{m} \times S_{n}$. For any ${\epsilon,\delta} > 0$, there exists some
$F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}^{W}$, such
that]{#S3.Thmtheorem6.p1.4.4 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\text{𝑀𝑒𝑎𝑠}\operatorname{}\left( \left\{ {{(G,H)} \in X}:{{\|{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} - {\Phi_{\text{𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑢}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}}\|} > \delta} \right\} \right)} < \epsilon}.$$   
  -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
:::
:::

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem7 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_corollary}
###### [[Corollary 3.7]{#S3.Thmtheorem7.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S3.Thmtheorem7.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#corollary-3.7. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S3.Thmtheorem7.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
$\mathcal{D} \subset {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}} \subset {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
be a finite dataset. For any $\delta > 0$, there exists some
$F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}^{W}$, such
that]{#S3.Thmtheorem7.p1.3.3 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{{\|{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} - {\Phi_{\text{𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑢}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}}\|} < \delta},{{\forall{(G,H)}} \in \mathcal{D}}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {#S4 .section .ltx_section .ltx_indent_first}
## [4. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_section}Sketch of proof {#sketch-of-proof .ltx_title .ltx_title_section}

::: {#S4.p1 .ltx_para}
In this section, we will present a sketch of our proof lines and provide
examples to show the intuitions. The full proof lines are presented in
the appendix.
:::

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px1 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Separation power {#separation-power .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p1 .ltx_para}
Separation power of GNN is closely related to the graph isomorphism
problem. To identify whether two given graphs are isomorphic, one may
apply the Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) test
\[[weisfeiler1968reduction]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\], a widely studied method that can distinguish a broad
class of graphs. It has been shown in the literature that the separation
power of GNN is actually equal to the standard WL test. Thus, we present
some concepts of the WL test before analyzing GNN.
:::

<figure id="alg1" class="ltx_float ltx_float_algorithm ltx_framed_top">
<div id="alg1.5" class="ltx_listing">
<div id="alg1.l1" class="ltx_listingline">
<span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_listingline"><span id="alg1.l1.1.1.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:80%;">1:</span></span>A graph instance
<span
class="math inline">(<em>G</em>,<em>H</em>) ∈ 𝒢<sub><em>m</em>, <em>n</em></sub> × ℋ<sub><em>m</em></sub><sup><em>V</em></sup> × ℋ<sub><em>n</em></sub><sup><em>W</em></sup></span>
and iteration limit <span class="math inline"><em>L</em> &gt; 0</span>.
</div>
<div id="alg1.l2" class="ltx_listingline">
<span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_listingline"><span id="alg1.l2.1.1.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:80%;">2:</span></span>Initialize with
<span
class="math inline"><em>C</em><sub><em>i</em></sub><sup>0, <em>V</em></sup> = HASH<sub>0, <em>V</em></sub> (<em>h</em><sub><em>i</em></sub><sup><em>V</em></sup>)</span>,
<span
class="math inline"><em>C</em><sub><em>j</em></sub><sup>0, <em>W</em></sup> = HASH<sub>0, <em>W</em></sub> (<em>h</em><sub><em>j</em></sub><sup><em>W</em></sup>)</span>.
</div>
<div id="alg1.l3" class="ltx_listingline">
<span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_listingline"><span id="alg1.l3.1.1.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:80%;">3:</span></span><span
id="alg1.l3.2" class="ltx_text ltx_font_bold">for</span> <span
class="math inline"><em>l</em> = 1, 2, ⋯, <em>L</em></span> <span
id="alg1.l3.3" class="ltx_text ltx_font_bold">do</span>
</div>
<div id="alg1.l4" class="ltx_listingline">
<span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_listingline"><span id="alg1.l4.1.1.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:80%;">4:</span></span>     <span
class="math inline">$C_{i}^{l,V} =
{\text{HASH}_{l,V}\operatorname{}\left( C_{i}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum_{j =
1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left(
C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} \right)}} \right)}$</span>.
</div>
<div id="alg1.l5" class="ltx_listingline">
<span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_listingline"><span id="alg1.l5.1.1.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:80%;">5:</span></span>     <span
class="math inline">$C_{j}^{l,W} =
{\text{HASH}_{l,W}\operatorname{}\left( C_{j}^{{l - 1},W},{\sum_{i =
1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,V}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left(
C_{i}^{{l - 1},V} \right)}} \right)}$</span>.
</div>
<div id="alg1.l6" class="ltx_listingline">
<span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_listingline"><span id="alg1.l6.1.1.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:80%;">6:</span></span><span
id="alg1.l6.2" class="ltx_text ltx_font_bold">end</span> <span
id="alg1.l6.3" class="ltx_text ltx_font_bold">for</span>
</div>
<div id="alg1.l7" class="ltx_listingline">
<span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_listingline"><span id="alg1.l7.1.1.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:80%;">7:</span></span><span
id="alg1.l7.2" class="ltx_text ltx_font_bold">return</span> The
multisets containing all colors <span
class="math inline">{{<em>C</em><sub><em>i</em></sub><sup><em>L</em>, <em>V</em></sup>}}<sub><em>i</em> = 0</sub><sup><em>m</em></sup>, {{<em>C</em><sub><em>j</em></sub><sup><em>L</em>, <em>W</em></sup>}}<sub><em>j</em> = 0</sub><sup><em>n</em></sup></span>.
</div>
</div>
<figcaption><span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_float"><span id="alg1.4.1.1"
class="ltx_text ltx_font_bold">Algorithm 1</span> </span> The WL test
for LP-Graphs (denoted by <span
class="math inline"><em>W</em><em>L</em><sub><em>L</em><em>P</em></sub></span>)</figcaption>
</figure>

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p2 .ltx_para}
To apply the WL test on LP-graphs, we describe a modified WL test in
Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
that is slightly different with the standard WL test. In
Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
the hash functions are injective mappings, and multisets are collections
of elements that allow multiple appearance of the same element.
$\{{\{\}}\}$ will be used throughout this paper to denote the multisets.
We denote Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
by ${WL}_{LP}\operatorname{}{( \cdot )}$, and we say that two LP-graphs
${(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ *can be distinguished by
Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}*
if and only if there exist a positive integer $L$ and injective hash
functions ${\{{HASH}_{l,V},{HASH}_{l,W}\}}_{l = 0}^{L}$ such that

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{{WL}_{LP}\operatorname{}\left( {(G,H)},L \right)} \neq {{WL}_{LP}\operatorname{}\left( {(\hat{G},\hat{H})},L \right)}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
:::

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p3 .ltx_para}
Unfortunately, similar with the standard WL test (see e.g.
\[[douglas2011weisfeiler]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\] and references therein), Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
does not perfectly solve the graph isomorphism problem on all LP-graphs.
There exist infinitely many pairs of non-isomorphic LP-graphs that
cannot be distinguished by Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
Figure [[2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
provide such an example.
:::

<div class="ltx_flex_break">

</div>

<span id="S4.F2.2"
class="ltx_inline-para ltx_minipage ltx_flex_size_4 ltx_align_middle"
style="width:112.7pt;"> <span id="S4.F2.2.p1" class="ltx_para"> <span

class="ltx_eqn_cell ltx_align_center"><span
class="math display">$$\begin{array}{cl}
\min &amp; {{x_{1} + x_{2} + x_{3} + x_{4}},} \\
\text{s.t.} &amp; {{{x_{1} + x_{2}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{2} + x_{3}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{3} + x_{4}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{4} + x_{1}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{j} \geq 1},{1 \leq j \leq 4}}.}
\end{array}$$</span></span> <span
class="ltx_eqn_cell ltx_eqn_center_padright"></span></span></span>
</span> </span></span>
</div>
<div class="ltx_flex_cell ltx_flex_size_4">
<span id="S4.F2.3"
class="ltx_inline-para ltx_minipage ltx_flex_size_4 ltx_align_middle"

class="math display">$$\begin{array}{cl}
\min &amp; {{x_{1} + x_{2} + x_{3} + x_{4}},} \\
\text{s.t.} &amp; {{{x_{1} + x_{2}} \leq 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{2} + x_{1}} \leq 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{3} + x_{4}} \leq 1},} \\

<span id="S4.F2.8"
class="ltx_inline-para ltx_minipage ltx_flex_size_4 ltx_align_middle"
style="width:112.7pt;"> <span id="S4.F2.8.p1" class="ltx_para"> <span
id="S4.Ex7" class="ltx_equation ltx_eqn_table"> <span><span
class="ltx_equation ltx_eqn_row ltx_align_baseline"> <span

class="math display">$$\begin{array}{cl}
\min &amp; {{x_{1} + x_{2} + x_{3} + x_{4}},} \\
\text{s.t.} &amp; {{{x_{1} + x_{2}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{2} + x_{1}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{3} + x_{4}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{4} + x_{3}} = 1},} \\
&amp; {{{x_{j} \leq 1},{1 \leq j \leq 4}}.}
