<figcaption><span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_figure"><span id="S4.F2.14.3.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:90%;">Figure 2</span>. </span><span
id="S4.F2.12.2" class="ltx_text" style="font-size:90%;">LP-graphs that
cannot be distinguished by the WL test since neighbor information of
<span class="math inline">{<em>v</em><sub><em>i</em></sub>}</span> and
<span class="math inline">{<em>w</em><sub><em>j</em></sub>}</span> in
the two graphs is equal. Based on the graph pair, we construct three
pairs of LPs that are both infeasible, both unbounded, both feasible
bounded with the same optimal solution,
respectively.</span></figcaption>
</figure>

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p4 .ltx_para}
However, for two LP-graphs that cannot be distinguished by
Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
they must share some common characteristics even if they are not
isomorphic. For example, let us consider the $6$ LP problems in Figure
[[2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
In each of the three columns, the two non-isomorphic LP problems cannot
be distinguished by the WL test. It can be checked that the two problems
in the same column share some common characteristics. More specifically,
both problems in the first column are infeasible; both problems in the
second column are feasible but unbounded; both problems in the third
column are feasible and bounded with $({1/2},{1/2},{1/2},{1/2})$ as the
optimal solution with the smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm. Actually, this
phenomenon does not only happen on the above instances, but also serves
as an universal principle for all LP instances. We summarize the results
in the following theorem:
:::

::: {#S4.Thmtheorem1 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem 4.1]{#S4.Thmtheorem1.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S4.Thmtheorem1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-4.1. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S4.Thmtheorem1.p1 .ltx_para}
[If
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
are not distinguishable by Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
then ]{#S4.Thmtheorem1.p1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
     $${{{\Phi_{\text{𝑓𝑒𝑎𝑠}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\Phi_{\text{𝑓𝑒𝑎𝑠}}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}},{{\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

[Furthermore, if
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\Phi_{\text{𝑜𝑏𝑗}}^{- 1}\operatorname{}{({\mathbb{R}})}}$,
it holds that
${\Phi_{\text{𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑢}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\sigma_{W}\operatorname{}{({\Phi_{\text{𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑢}}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}})}}$
for some $\sigma_{W} \in S_{n}$.]{#S4.Thmtheorem1.p1.4.3 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p5 .ltx_para}
In other words, the above theorem guarantees the sufficient power of the
WL test for separating LP problems with different characteristics,
including feasibility, optimal objective value, and optimal solution
with smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm (up to permutation). This combined with
the following theorem, which states the equivalence of the separation
powers of the WL test and GNNs, yields that GNNs also have sufficient
separation power for LP-graphs in the above sense.
:::

::: {#S4.Thmtheorem2 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem 4.2]{#S4.Thmtheorem2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S4.Thmtheorem2.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-4.2. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S4.Thmtheorem2.p1 .ltx_para}
[For any
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$,
the followings are equivalent:]{#S4.Thmtheorem2.p1.1.1 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_italic}

-   [[(i)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S4.I1.ix1}

    ::: {#S4.I1.ix1.p1 .ltx_para}
    $(G,H)$[ and ]{#S4.I1.ix1.p1.2.1 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}$(\hat{G},\hat{H})$[ are not distinguishable by
    Algorithm ]{#S4.I1.ix1.p1.2.2 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}[[1]{.ltx_text
    .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref
    .ltx_font_italic}[.]{#S4.I1.ix1.p1.2.3 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
-   [[(ii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S4.I1.ix2}

    ::: {#S4.I1.ix2.p1 .ltx_para}
    ${{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {F\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}},{{\forall F} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}}$[.]{#S4.I1.ix2.p1.1.1
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
-   [[(iii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#S4.I1.ix3}

    ::: {#S4.I1.ix3.p1 .ltx_para}
    [For any ]{#S4.I1.ix3.p1.3.1 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}$F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}^{W}$[, there
    exists ]{#S4.I1.ix3.p1.3.2 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}$\sigma_{W} \in S_{n}$[ such that
    ]{#S4.I1.ix3.p1.3.3 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}${F_{W}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\sigma_{W}\operatorname{}{({F_{W}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}})}}$[.]{#S4.I1.ix3.p1.3.4
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
:::
:::

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px1.p6 .ltx_para}
Theorem [[4.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem2 "Theorem 4.2. ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
extends the results in \[[xu2019powerful]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [azizian2020expressive]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}, [geerts2022expressiveness]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\] to the case with the modified WL
test (Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
and LP-graphs.
:::
:::

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px2 .section .ltx_paragraph .ltx_indentfirst}
#### Representation power {#representation-power .ltx_title .ltx_font_bold .ltx_title_paragraph}

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px2.p1 .ltx_para}
Based on the separation power of GNNs, one is able to investigate the
representation/approximation power of GNNs. To prove Theorems
[[3.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem2 "Theorem 3.2. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
[[3.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem4 "Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
and [[3.6]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem6 "Theorem 3.6. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
we first determine the closure of $\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}$ or
$\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}^{W}$ in the space of invariant/equivariant
continuous functions with respect to the $\sup$-norm, which is also
named as the *universal approximation*. The result of
$\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}$ is stated as follows, where
$\mathcal{C}\operatorname{}{(X,{\mathbb{R}})}$ is the collection/algebra
of all real-valued continuous function on $X$. The result of
$\mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}^{W}$ can be found in the appendix.
:::

::: {#S4.Thmtheorem3 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem 4.3]{#S4.Thmtheorem3.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#S4.Thmtheorem3.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-4.3. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#S4.Thmtheorem3.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
$X \subset {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$
be a compact set. For any
$\Phi \in {\mathcal{C}\operatorname{}{(X,{\mathbb{R}})}}$ that satisfies
${\Phi\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\Phi\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}}$
for all ${(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ that are not distinguishable by
Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
and for any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists
$F \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}$ such that]{#S4.Thmtheorem3.p1.6.6
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\sup\limits_{{(G,H)} \in X}{|{{\Phi\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} - {F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}}|}} < \epsilon}.$$   
  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
:::
:::

::: {#S4.SS0.SSS0.Px2.p2 .ltx_para}
Theorem [[4.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem3 "Theorem 4.3. ‣ Representation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
can be viewed as an LP-graph version of results in
\[[azizian2020expressive]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
[geerts2022expressiveness]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. Roughly speaking, graph neural networks can
approximate any invariant continuous function with arbitrarily small
error. Although our target mappings
$\Phi_{\text{feas}},\Phi_{\text{obj}},\Phi_{\text{solu}}$ are not
continuous, we prove (in appendix) that they are measurable. Applying
Lusin's theorem \[[evans2018measure]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]\*Theorem 1.14, we show that GNN can be arbitrarily
close to the target mappings except for a small domain.
:::
:::
:::

::: {#S5 .section .ltx_section .ltx_indent_first}
## [5. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_section}Numerical Experiments {#numerical-experiments .ltx_title .ltx_title_section}

::: {#S5.p1 .ltx_para}
We present the numerical results that validate our theoretical results
in this section. We generate LP instances with $m = 10$ and $n = 50$
that are possibly infeasible or feasible and bounded. To check whether
GNN can predict feasibility, we generate three data sets with
$100,500,2500$ independent LP instances respectively, and call the
solver wrapped in [scipy.optimize.linprog]{#S5.p1.5.1 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_typewriter} to get the feasibility, optimal value and an
optimal solution for each generated LP. To generate enough feasible and
bounded LPs to check whether GNN can approximate the optimal objective
value and optimal solution, we follow the same approach as before to
generate LP randomly and discard those infeasible LPs until the number
of LPs reach our requirement. We train GNNs to fit the three LP
characteristics by minimizing the distance between GNN-output and those
solver-generated answers. The building and the training of the GNNs are
implemented using [TensorFlow]{#S5.p1.5.2 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_typewriter} and the codes are modified
from[^3^[[^3^[3]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_note}Our codes are modified from
[https://github.com/ds4dm/learn2branch](https://github.com/ds4dm/learn2branch){.ltx_ref
.ltx_url .ltx_font_typewriter target="_blank"} and we will release
codes.]{.ltx_note_content}]{.ltx_note_outer}]{#footnote3 .ltx_note
.ltx_role_footnote} \[[gasse2019exact]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. We set $L = 2$ for all GNNs and those learnable
functions
$f_{in}^{V},f_{in}^{W},f_{\text{out}},f_{\text{out}}^{W},{\{ f_{l}^{V},f_{l}^{W},g_{l}^{V},g_{l}^{W}\}}_{l = 0}^{L}$
are all parameterized with MLPs. Details can be found in the appendix.
Our results are reported in Figure [[3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F3 "Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
:::

<figcaption><span class="ltx_tag ltx_tag_figure"><span id="S5.F3.8.4.1"
class="ltx_text" style="font-size:90%;">Figure 3</span>. </span><span
id="S5.F3.6.3" class="ltx_text" style="font-size:90%;">GNN can
approximate <span class="math inline"><em>Φ</em><sub>feas</sub></span>,
<span class="math inline"><em>Φ</em><sub>obj</sub></span>, and <span
class="math inline"><em>Φ</em><sub>solu</sub></span></span></figcaption>
</figure>

::: {#S5.p2 .ltx_para}
All the errors reported in Figure [[3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F3 "Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
are training errors since generalization is out of the scope of this
paper. In Figure [[2(a)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf1 "2(a) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
the "rate of errors" means the proportion of instances with
${\mathbb{I}}_{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} > {1/2}} \neq {\Phi_{\text{feas}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}$.
This metric exactly equals to zeros as long as the number of parameters
in GNN is large enough, which directly validates Corollary
[[3.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem3 "Corollary 3.3. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
With the three curves in Figure [[2(a)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf1 "2(a) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
combined together, we conclude that such principle does not violate as
the number of samples increases. This consists with Theorem
[[3.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem2 "Theorem 3.2. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
Mean squared errors in Figures [[2(b)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf2 "2(b) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
and [[2(c)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf3 "2(c) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
are respectively defined as
${\mathbb{E}}_{(G,H)}\operatorname{}{|{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} - {\Phi_{\text{feas}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}}|}^{2}$
and
${\mathbb{E}}_{(G,H)}\operatorname{}{\|{{F\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} - {\Phi_{\text{feas}}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}}}\|}^{2}$.
Therefore, Figures [[2(b)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf2 "2(b) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
and [[2(c)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf3 "2(c) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
validates Theorems [[3.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem4 "Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
and [[3.6]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem6 "Theorem 3.6. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
respectively. Note that all the instances used in Figure
[[2(b)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf2 "2(b) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
are feasible and bounded. Thus, Figure [[2(b)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf2 "2(b) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
actually only validates ([[3.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.E2 "3.2 ‣ Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
in Theorem [[3.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem4 "Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
However, due to the fact that feasibility of an LP is equal to the
boundedness of its dual problem, one may dualize each LP and use the
conclusion of Figure [[2(a)]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S5.F2.sf1 "2(a) ‣ Figure 3 ‣ 5. Numerical Experiments ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
to validate ([[3.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.E1 "3.1 ‣ Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
in Theorem [[3.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S3.Thmtheorem4 "Theorem 3.4. ‣ Topology and measure ‣ 3. Main results ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
:::
:::

::: {#S6 .section .ltx_section .ltx_indent_first}
## [6. ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_section}Conclusions {#conclusions .ltx_title .ltx_title_section}

::: {#S6.p1 .ltx_para}
In this work, we show that graph neural networks, as well as the WL
test, have sufficient separation power to distinguish linear programming
problems with different characteristics. In addition, GNNs can
approximate LP feasibility, optimal objective value, and optimal
solution with arbitrarily small errors on compact domains or finite
datasets. These results guarantee that GNN is a proper class of machine
learning models to represent linear programs, and hence contribute to
the theoretical foundation in the learning-to-optimize community. Future
directions include investing the representation power of graph neural
networks for mixed-integer linear programming (MILP), which has been
observed with promising experimental results in the literature.
:::

::: {#bib .section .ltx_bibliography}
## References {#references .ltx_title .ltx_title_bibliography}
:::
:::

::: {#A1 .section .ltx_appendix .ltx_indent_first}
## [Appendix A ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_appendix}Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) Test and color refinement {#appendix-a-weisfeiler-lehman-wl-test-and-color-refinement .ltx_title .ltx_title_appendix}

::: {#A1.p1 .ltx_para}
The WL test can be viewed as a coloring refinement procedure if there
are no collisions of hash functions and their weighted averages. More
specifically, each vertex is colored initially according to the group it
belongs to and its feature -- two vertices have the same color if and
only if they are in the same vertex group and have the same feature. The
initial colors are denoted as
$C_{1}^{0,V},C_{2}^{0,V},\ldots,C_{m}^{0,V},C_{1}^{0,W},C_{2}^{0,W},\ldots,C_{n}^{0,W}$.
Then at iteration $l$, the set of vertices with the same color at
iteration $l - 1$ are further partitioned into several subsets according
to the colors of their neighbours -- two vertices $v_{i}$ and
$v_{i^{\prime}}$ are in the same subset if and only if
$C_{i}^{{l - 1},V} = C_{i^{\prime}}^{{l - 1},V}$ and for any
$C \in {\{ C_{j}^{{l - 1},W}:{1 \leq j \leq n}\}}$,

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\sum\limits_{C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C}E_{i,j}} = {\sum\limits_{C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C}E_{i^{\prime},j}}},$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

and it is similar for vertices $w_{j}$ and $w_{j^{\prime}}$. After such
partition/refinement, vertices are associated with the same color if and
only if they are in the same subset, which is the coloring at iteration
$l$. This procedure is terminated if the refinement is trivial, meaning
that no sets with the same color are partitioned into at least two
subsets, i.e., the coloring is stable. For more information about color
refinement, we refer to \[[berkholz2017tight]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}, [arvind2015power]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}, [arvind2017graph]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\].
:::

::: {#A1.p2 .ltx_para}
We then discuss the stable coloring that Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
will converge to, for which we made the following definition, where
$\mathcal{S} = {\{ S_{1},S_{2},\ldots,S_{s}\}}$ is called a partition of
a set $S$ if ${S_{1} \cup S_{2} \cup \cdots \cup S_{s}} = S$ and
${{S_{i} \cap S_{i^{\prime}}} = \varnothing},{{\forall 1} \leq i < i^{\prime} \leq s}$.
:::

::: {#A1.Thmtheorem1 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_definition}
###### [[Definition A.1]{#A1.Thmtheorem1.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[ ]{#A1.Thmtheorem1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}(Stable Partition Pair of Vertices)[.]{#A1.Thmtheorem1.3.3 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#definition-a.1-stable-partition-pair-of-vertices. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A1.Thmtheorem1.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let $G = {({V \cup W},E)}$ be a weighted bipartite graph with
$V = {\{ v_{1},v_{2},\ldots,v_{n}\}}$,
$W = {\{ w_{1},w_{2},\ldots,w_{n}\}}$, and vertex features
$H = {(h_{1}^{V},h_{2}^{V},\ldots,h_{m}^{V},h_{1}^{W},h_{2}^{W},\ldots,h_{n}^{W})}$,
and let $\mathcal{I} = {\{ I_{1},I_{2},\ldots,I_{s}\}}$ and
$\mathcal{J} = {\{ J_{1},J_{2},\ldots,J_{t}\}}$ be partitions of
$\{ 1,2,\ldots,m\}$ and $\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}$, respectively. We say that
$(\mathcal{I},\mathcal{J})$ is a stable partition pair of vertices for
the graph $G$ if the followings are satisfied:]{#A1.Thmtheorem1.p1.10.10
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}

-   [[(i)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A1.I1.ix1}

    ::: {#A1.I1.ix1.p1 .ltx_para}
    $h_{i}^{V} = h_{i^{\prime}}^{V}$[ holds if ]{#A1.I1.ix1.p1.3.1
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}${i,i^{\prime}} \in I_{p}$[ for some
    ]{#A1.I1.ix1.p1.3.2 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}$p \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,s\}}$[.]{#A1.I1.ix1.p1.3.3
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
-   [[(ii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A1.I1.ix2}

    ::: {#A1.I1.ix2.p1 .ltx_para}
    $h_{j}^{W} = h_{j^{\prime}}^{W}$[ holds if ]{#A1.I1.ix2.p1.3.1
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}${j,j^{\prime}} \in J_{q}$[ for some
    ]{#A1.I1.ix2.p1.3.2 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}$q \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$[.]{#A1.I1.ix2.p1.3.3
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
-   [[(iii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A1.I1.ix3}

    ::: {#A1.I1.ix3.p1 .ltx_para}
    [For any ]{#A1.I1.ix3.p1.4.1 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}$p \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,s\}}$[, ]{#A1.I1.ix3.p1.4.2
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}$q \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$[, and
    ]{#A1.I1.ix3.p1.4.3 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}${i,i^{\prime}} \in I_{p}$[, ]{#A1.I1.ix3.p1.4.4
    .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}${\sum_{j \in J_{q}}E_{i,j}} = {\sum_{j \in J_{q}}E_{i^{\prime},j}}$[.]{#A1.I1.ix3.p1.4.5
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
-   [[(iv)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A1.I1.ix4}

    ::: {#A1.I1.ix4.p1 .ltx_para}
    [For any ]{#A1.I1.ix4.p1.4.1 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}$p \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,s\}}$[, ]{#A1.I1.ix4.p1.4.2
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}$q \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$[, and
    ]{#A1.I1.ix4.p1.4.3 .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}${j,j^{\prime}} \in J_{q}$[, ]{#A1.I1.ix4.p1.4.4
    .ltx_text
    .ltx_font_italic}${\sum_{i \in I_{p}}E_{i,j}} = {\sum_{i \in I_{p}}E_{i,j^{\prime}}}$[.]{#A1.I1.ix4.p1.4.5
    .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
    :::
:::
:::

::: {#A1.p3 .ltx_para}
We denote $(\mathcal{I}^{l},\mathcal{J}^{l})$ as the partition pair
corresponding the coloring at iteration $l$ of Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
Suppose that there are no collisions. Then it is clear that
$(\mathcal{I}^{l + 1},\mathcal{J}^{l + 1})$ is finer than
$(\mathcal{I}^{l},\mathcal{J}^{l})$, denoted as
${(\mathcal{I}^{l + 1},\mathcal{J}^{l + 1})} \preceq {(\mathcal{I}^{l},\mathcal{J}^{l})}$,
which means that for any $I \in \mathcal{I}^{l + 1}$ and any
$J \in \mathcal{J}^{l + 1}$, there exist
$I^{\prime} \in \mathcal{I}^{l}$ and $J^{\prime} \in \mathcal{J}^{l}$
such that $I \subset I^{\prime}$ and $J \subset J^{\prime}$. In
addition,
${(\mathcal{I}^{l + 1},\mathcal{J}^{l + 1})} = {(\mathcal{I}^{l},\mathcal{J}^{l})}$
if and only if $(\mathcal{I}^{l},\mathcal{J}^{l})$ is a stable partition
pair of vertices. Note that there is at most
$O\operatorname{}{({{|V|} + {|W|}})}$ iterations leading to strictly
finer partition pair, i.e,
${(\mathcal{I}^{l + 1},\mathcal{J}^{l + 1})} \preceq {(\mathcal{I}^{l},\mathcal{J}^{l})}$
but
${(\mathcal{I}^{l + 1},\mathcal{J}^{l + 1})} \neq {(\mathcal{I}^{l},\mathcal{J}^{l})}$.
We can immediately obtain the following result:
:::

::: {#A1.Thmtheorem2 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_theorem}
###### [[Theorem A.2]{#A1.Thmtheorem2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A1.Thmtheorem2.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#theorem-a.2. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A1.Thmtheorem2.p1 .ltx_para}
[If there are no collision of hash functions and their weighted
averages, then Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
terminates at a stable partition pair of vertices in
$O\operatorname{}{({{|V|} + {|W|}})}$
iterations.]{#A1.Thmtheorem2.p1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A1.p4 .ltx_para}
Furthermore, for every
${(G,H)} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$,
the coarsest stable partition pair of vertices exists and is unique,
which can be proved using techniques similar to the proof of
\[[berkholz2017tight]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]\*Proposition 3. Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
terminates at the unique coarsest stable partition pair. This is because
that the coloring gin each iteration of Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
is always coarser than the unique coarsest stable partition pair (see
\[[berkholz2017tight]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]\*Proposition 2).
:::
:::

::: {#A2 .section .ltx_appendix .ltx_indent_first}
## [Appendix B ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_appendix}The separation power of the WL test {#appendix-b-the-separation-power-of-the-wl-test .ltx_title .ltx_title_appendix}

::: {#A2.p1 .ltx_para}
This section gives the proof and some corollaries of Theorem
[[4.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem1 "Theorem 4.1. ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
First we present some definitions and lemmas, from which the proof of
Theorem [[4.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem1 "Theorem 4.1. ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
can be immediately derived.
:::

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem1 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_definition}
###### [[Definition B.1]{#A2.Thmtheorem1.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A2.Thmtheorem1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#definition-b.1. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem1.p1 .ltx_para}
[Given
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$,
we say that $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$ can be distinguished by the
WL test if there exists some $L \in {\mathbb{N}}$ and some choices of
hash functions, $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{0,V}$, $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{0,W}$,
$\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,V}$, $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,W}$, $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,V}^{\prime}$,
and $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,W}^{\prime}$, for $l = {1,2,\ldots,L}$, such that
the multisets of colors at the $L$-th iteration of the WL test are
different for $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$. Let $\sim$ be an
equivalence relationship on
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}$
defined via: ${(G,H)} \sim {(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ if and only if they can
not be distinguished by the WL test.]{#A2.Thmtheorem1.p1.17.17 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A2.p2 .ltx_para}
It is clear that ${(G,H)} \sim {(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ if they are
isometric, i.e., there exist two permutations,
$\sigma_{V}:{{\{ 1,2,\ldots,m\}}\rightarrow{\{ 1,2,\ldots,m\}}}$ and
$\sigma_{W}:{{\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}}\rightarrow{\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}}}$, such
that
$E_{{\sigma_{V}\operatorname{}{(i)}},{\sigma_{W}\operatorname{}{(j)}}} = {\hat{E}}_{i,j}$,
$h_{\sigma_{V}\operatorname{}{(i)}}^{V} = {\hat{h}}_{i}^{V}$, and
$h_{\sigma_{W}\operatorname{}{(j)}}^{W} = {\hat{h}}_{j}^{W}$, for any
$i \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,m\}}$ and $j \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}}$. However,
not every pair of WL-indistinguishable graphs consists of isometric
ones; see Figure [[2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
for an example. However, for LP problems that cannot be distinguished by
the WL test will share some common properties, even if their associated
graphs are not isomorphic.
:::

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem2 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_lemma}
###### [[Lemma B.2]{#A2.Thmtheorem2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A2.Thmtheorem2.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#lemma-b.2. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem2.p1 .ltx_para}
[If two weighted bipartite graphs with vertex features corresponding to
two LP problems are indistinguishable by the WL test, then either both
problems are feasible or both are infeasible. In other words, the WL
test can distinguish two LP problems if one of them is feasible while
the other one is infeasible.]{#A2.Thmtheorem2.p1.1.1 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A2.2 .ltx_proof}
###### Proof of Lemma [[B.2]{.ltx_text .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem2 "Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}. {#proof-of-lemma-b.2. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_font_italic .ltx_title_proof}

::: {#A2.1.p1 .ltx_para}
Let us consider two LP problems:

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(B.1)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $$\begin{aligned}                                                         
                                                           \min\limits_{x \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}} & {{c^{\top}\operatorname{}x},} \\   
                                                           \text{s.t.} & {{{{{A\operatorname{}x} \circ b},l} \leq x \leq u},}        
                                                           \end{aligned}$$                                                           
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

and

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(B.2)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $$\begin{aligned}                                                                                                            
                                                           \min\limits_{x \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}} & {{{\hat{c}}^{\top}\operatorname{}x},} \\                                              
                                                           \text{s.t.} & {{{{\hat{A}\operatorname{}x\operatorname{}\hat{\circ}\operatorname{}\hat{b}},\hat{l}} \leq x \leq \hat{u}}.}   
                                                           \end{aligned}$$                                                                                                              
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

Let $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$, where $G = {({V \cup W},E)}$ and
$\hat{G} = {({V \cup W},\hat{E})}$, be the weighted bipartite graphs
with vertex features corresponding to ([[B.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E1 "B.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
and ([[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E2 "B.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),
respectively.
:::

::: {#A2.2.p2 .ltx_para}
Since ${(G,H)} \sim {(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$, for some choice of hash
functions with no collision during Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
for $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$, Theorem [[A.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A1.Thmtheorem2 "Theorem A.2. ‣ Appendix A Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) Test and color refinement ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
guarantees that Algorithm [[1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
outputs the same stable coloring for $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$ up
to permutation. More specifically, after doing some permutation, there
exist $\mathcal{I} = {\{ I_{1},I_{2},\ldots,I_{s}\}}$ and
$\mathcal{J} = {\{ J_{1},J_{2},\ldots,J_{t}\}}$ that are partitions of
$\{ 1,2,\ldots,m\}$ and $\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}$, respectively, such that the
followings hold:

-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A2.I1.i1}

    ::: {#A2.I1.i1.p1 .ltx_para}
    ${(b_{i}, \circ_{i})} = {({\hat{b}}_{i},{\hat{\circ}}_{i})}$ and is
    independent of $i \in I_{p}$, for any $p \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,s\}}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A2.I1.i2}

    ::: {#A2.I1.i2.p1 .ltx_para}
    ${(l_{j},u_{j})} = {({\hat{l}}_{j},{\hat{u}}_{j})}$ and is
    independent of $j \in J_{q}$ for any $q \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A2.I1.i3}

    ::: {#A2.I1.i3.p1 .ltx_para}
    For any $p \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,s\}}$ and $q \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$,
    ${\sum_{j \in J_{q}}A_{i,j}} = {\sum_{j \in J_{q}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}}$
    and is independent of $i \in I_{p}$.
    :::
-   [[•]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A2.I1.i4}

    ::: {#A2.I1.i4.p1 .ltx_para}
    For any $p \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,s\}}$ and $q \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$,
    ${\sum_{i \in I_{p}}A_{i,j}} = {\sum_{i \in I_{p}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}}$
    and is independent of $j \in J_{q}$.
    :::

Suppose that the problem ([[B.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E1 "B.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
is feasible with $x \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}$ be some point in the feasible
region. Define $y \in {\mathbb{R}}^{t}$ via
$y_{q} = {\frac{1}{|J_{q}|}\operatorname{}{\sum_{j \in J_{q}}x_{j}}}$
and $\hat{x} \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}$ via ${\hat{x}}_{j} = y_{q}$,
$j \in J_{q}$. Fix any $p \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,s\}}$ and some
$i_{0} \in I_{p}$. It holds for any $i \in I_{p}$ that

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{{A_{i,j}\operatorname{}x_{j}} \circ_{i}b_{i}}},\text{i.e.},{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{{A_{i,j}\operatorname{}x_{j}} \circ_{i_{0}}b_{i_{0}}}}}},$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

which implies that

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
     $${{\frac{1}{|I_{p}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{i \in I_{p}}{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{A_{i,j}\operatorname{}x_{j}}}}}} = {\frac{1}{|I_{p}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{{\left( {\sum\limits_{i \in I_{p}}A_{i,j}} \right)\operatorname{}x_{j}} \circ_{i_{0}}b_{i_{0}}}}}}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

Notice that $\sum_{i \in I_{p}}A_{i,j}$ is constant for $j \in J_{q}$,
${\forall q} \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$. Let us denote
$\alpha_{q} = {\sum_{i \in I_{p}}A_{i,j}} = {\sum_{i \in I_{p}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}}$
for any $j \in J_{q}$. Then it holds that

  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\frac{1}{|I_{p}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{\alpha_{q}\operatorname{}x_{j}}}}} = {\frac{1}{|I_{p}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{\alpha_{q}\operatorname{}y_{q}}}}} = {\frac{1}{|I_{p}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{{\left( {\sum\limits_{i \in I_{p}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}} \right)\operatorname{}y_{q}} \circ_{i_{0}}b_{i_{0}}}}}}}.$$   
  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

Note that

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
     $${{\frac{1}{|I_{p}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{\left( {\sum\limits_{i \in I_{p}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}} \right)\operatorname{}y_{q}}}}} = {\frac{1}{|I_{p}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{i \in I_{p}}{\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\left( {\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}} \right)\operatorname{}y_{q}}}}}},$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

and that $\sum_{j \in J_{q}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}$ is constant for
$i \in I_{p}$. So one can conclude that

  -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{{\hat{A}}_{i,j}\operatorname{}{\hat{x}}_{j}}} = {\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{{\hat{A}}_{i,j}\operatorname{}{\hat{x}}_{j}}}} = {\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{{\left( {\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}{\hat{A}}_{i,j}} \right)\operatorname{}y_{q}} \circ_{i}b_{i}}}},{{\forall i} \in I_{p}}},$$   
  -- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

which leads to
$\hat{A}\operatorname{}\hat{x}\operatorname{}\hat{\circ}\operatorname{}\hat{b}$.
It can also be seen that $\hat{l} = l \leq \hat{x} \leq u \leq \hat{u}$.
Therefore, $\hat{x}$ is feasible for ([[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E2 "B.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}).
We have shown above that the feasibility of ([[B.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E1 "B.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
implies the feasibility of ([[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E2 "B.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}).
The inverse is also true by the same reasoning. Hence, we complete the
proof. ∎
:::
:::

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem3 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_lemma}
###### [[Lemma B.3]{#A2.Thmtheorem3.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A2.Thmtheorem3.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#lemma-b.3. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem3.p1 .ltx_para}
[If two weighted bipartite graphs with vertex features corresponding to
two LP problems are indistinguishable by the WL test, then these two
problems share the same optimal objective value (could be $\infty$ or
$- \infty$).]{#A2.Thmtheorem3.p1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A2.3 .ltx_proof}
###### Proof of Lemma [[B.3]{.ltx_text .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem3 "Lemma B.3. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}. {#proof-of-lemma-b.3. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_font_italic .ltx_title_proof}

::: {#A2.3.p1 .ltx_para}
If both problems are infeasible, then their optimal objective values are
both $\infty$. We then consider the case that both problems are
feasible. We use the same setting and notations as in
Lemma [[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem2 "Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
and in addition we have that $c_{j} = {\hat{c}}_{j}$, which is part of
$h_{j}^{W} = {\hat{h}}_{j}^{W}$, is independent of $j \in J_{q}$ for any
$q \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,t\}}$. Suppose that $x$ is an feasible solution to
the problem ([[B.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E1 "B.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
and let $\hat{x} \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}$ be defined via
${{\hat{x}}_{j} = {\frac{1}{|J_{q}|}\operatorname{}{\sum_{j^{\prime} \in J_{q}}x_{j^{\prime}}}}},{j \in J_{q}}$.
It is guaranteed by the proof of Lemma [[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem2 "Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
that $\hat{x}$ is a feasible solution to ([[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E2 "B.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}).
One can also see that
${c^{\top}\operatorname{}x} = {{\hat{c}}^{\top}\operatorname{}\hat{x}}$.
Since this holds for any feasible solution $x$ to ([[B.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E1 "B.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),
the optimal value of the objective function for ([[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E2 "B.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
is smaller than or equal to that for ([[B.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E1 "B.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}).
The inverse is also true and the proof is completed. ∎
:::
:::

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem4 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_lemma}
###### [[Lemma B.4]{#A2.Thmtheorem4.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A2.Thmtheorem4.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#lemma-b.4. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem4.p1 .ltx_para}
[Suppose that two weighted bipartite graphs with vertex features
corresponding to two LP problems are indistinguishable by the WL test
and the their optimal objective values are both finite. Then these two
problems have the same optimal solution with the smallest
$\ell_{2}$-norm, up to permutation.]{#A2.Thmtheorem4.p1.1.1 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A2.4 .ltx_proof}
###### Proof of Lemma [[B.4]{.ltx_text .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem4 "Lemma B.4. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}. {#proof-of-lemma-b.4. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_font_italic .ltx_title_proof}

::: {#A2.4.p1 .ltx_para}
We work with the same setting as in Lemma [[B.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem3 "Lemma B.3. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
where permutations have already been applied. Let $x$ and $x^{\prime}$
be the optimal solution to ([[B.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E1 "B.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
and ([[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E2 "B.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
with the smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm, respectively. (Recall that the
optimal solution to a LP problem with the smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm is
unique, see Remark [[2.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.Thmtheorem2 "Remark 2.2. ‣ Optimal objective value mapping ‣ 2.3. Revisiting question (P1) ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.)
Let $\hat{x} \in {\mathbb{R}}^{n}$ be defined via
${\hat{x}}_{j} = {\frac{1}{|J_{q}|}\operatorname{}{\sum_{j^{\prime} \in J_{q}}x_{j^{\prime}}}}$
for $j \in J_{q}$, $q = {1,2,\ldots,t}$. According to the arguments in
the proof of Lemma [[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem2 "Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
and Lemma [[B.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem3 "Lemma B.3. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
$\hat{x}$ is an optimal solution to ([[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E2 "B.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}).
The minimality of $x^{\prime}$ yields that

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(B.3)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${{\| x^{\prime}\|}_{2}^{2} \leq {\|\hat{x}\|}_{2}^{2} = {\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{{|J_{q}|}\operatorname{}\left( {\frac{1}{|J_{q}|}\operatorname{}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}x_{j}}} \right)^{2}}} = {\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\frac{1}{|J_{q}|}\operatorname{}\left( {\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}x_{j}} \right)^{2}}} \leq {\sum\limits_{q = 1}^{t}{\sum\limits_{j \in J_{q}}x_{j}^{2}}} = {\| x\|}_{2}^{2}},$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

which implies ${\| x^{\prime}\|} \leq {\| x\|}$. The converse
${\| x\|} \leq {\| x^{\prime}\|}$ is also true. Therefore, we must have
${\| x\|} = {\| x^{\prime}\|}$ and hence, the inequalities in
([[B.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.E3 "B.3 ‣ Proof of Lemma B.4. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
must hold as equalities. Then one can conclude that
$x_{j} = x_{j^{\prime}}$ for any ${j,j^{\prime}} \in J_{q}$ and
$q = {1,2,\ldots,t}$, which leads to $x = \hat{x}$. Furthermore, it
follows from ${\| x^{\prime}\|} = {\|\hat{x}\|}$ and the uniqueness of
$x^{\prime}$ (see Remark [[2.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.Thmtheorem2 "Remark 2.2. ‣ Optimal objective value mapping ‣ 2.3. Revisiting question (P1) ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
that $x^{\prime} = \hat{x} = x$, which completes the proof. ∎
:::
:::

::: {#A2.p3 .ltx_para}
One corollary one can see from the proof of Lemma [[B.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem4 "Lemma B.4. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
is that the components of the optimal solution with the smallest
$\ell_{2}$-norm must be the same if the two corresponding vertices have
the same color in the WL test.
:::

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem5 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_corollary}
###### [[Corollary B.5]{#A2.Thmtheorem5.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A2.Thmtheorem5.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#corollary-b.5. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem5.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let $(G,H)$ be a weighted bipartite graph with vertex features and let
$x$ be the optimal solution to the corresponding LP problem with the
smallest $\ell_{2}$-norm. Suppose that for some
${j,j^{\prime}} \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}}$, one has
$C_{j}^{l,W} = C_{j^{\prime}}^{l,W}$ for any $l \in {\mathbb{N}}$ and
any choices of hash functions, then
$x_{j} = x_{j^{\prime}}$.]{#A2.Thmtheorem5.p1.7.7 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A2.p4 .ltx_para}
Let us also define another equivalence relationship on
$\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}$
where colors of $w_{1},w_{2},\ldots,w_{j}$ with ordering (not just
multisets) are considered:
:::

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem6 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_definition}
###### [[Definition B.6]{#A2.Thmtheorem6.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A2.Thmtheorem6.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#definition-b.6. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem6.p1 .ltx_para}
[Given
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$,
$(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$ are not in the same equivalence class of
$\overset{W}{\sim}$ if and only if there exist some $L \in {\mathbb{N}}$
and some hash functions $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{0,V}$, $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{0,W}$,
$\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,V}$, $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,W}$, $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,V}^{\prime}$,
and $\text{𝐻𝐴𝑆𝐻}_{l,W}^{\prime}$, $l = {1,2,\ldots,L}$, such that
${\{{\{ C_{1}^{L,V},C_{2}^{L,V},\ldots,C_{m}^{L,V}\}}\}} \neq {\{{\{{\hat{C}}_{1}^{L,V},{\hat{C}}_{2}^{L,V},\ldots,{\hat{C}}_{m}^{L,V}\}}\}}$
or $C_{j}^{l,W} \neq {\hat{C}}_{j}^{l,W}$ for some
$j \in {\{ 1,2,\ldots,n\}}$.]{#A2.Thmtheorem6.p1.15.15 .ltx_text
.ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A2.p5 .ltx_para}
It is clear that ${(G,H)}\overset{W}{\sim}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ implies
${(G,H)} \sim {(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$. One can actually obtain a stronger
version of Lemma [[B.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem4 "Lemma B.4. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
given ${(G,H)}\overset{W}{\sim}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$.
:::

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem7 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_corollary}
###### [[Corollary B.7]{#A2.Thmtheorem7.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A2.Thmtheorem7.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#corollary-b.7. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A2.Thmtheorem7.p1 .ltx_para}
[Suppose that two weighted bipartite graphs with vertex features
corresponding to two LP problems, $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$,
satisfies that ${(G,H)}\overset{W}{\sim}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$. Then these
two problems have the same optimal solution with the smallest
$\ell_{2}$-norm.]{#A2.Thmtheorem7.p1.4.4 .ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A2.5 .ltx_proof}
###### Proof of Corollary [[B.7]{.ltx_text .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem7 "Corollary B.7. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}. {#proof-of-corollary-b.7. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_font_italic .ltx_title_proof}

::: {#A2.5.p1 .ltx_para}
The proof of Lemma [[B.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem4 "Lemma B.4. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
still applies with the difference that there is no permutation on
$\{ w_{1},w_{2},\ldots,w_{n}\}$. ∎
:::
:::

::: {#A2.6 .ltx_proof}
###### Proof of Theorem [[4.1]{.ltx_text .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem1 "Theorem 4.1. ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}. {#proof-of-theorem-4.1. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_font_italic .ltx_title_proof}

::: {#A2.6.p1 .ltx_para}
The proof of Theorem [[4.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem1 "Theorem 4.1. ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
follows immediately from Lemmas [[B.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem2 "Lemma B.2. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
[[B.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem3 "Lemma B.3. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}
and [[B.4]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A2.Thmtheorem4 "Lemma B.4. ‣ Appendix B The separation power of the WL test ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
∎
:::
:::
:::

::: {#A3 .section .ltx_appendix .ltx_indent_first}
## [Appendix C ]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_appendix}Separation power of graph neural networks {#appendix-c-separation-power-of-graph-neural-networks .ltx_title .ltx_title_appendix}

::: {#A3.p1 .ltx_para}
This section aims to prove Theorem [[4.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem2 "Theorem 4.2. ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref},
i.e., the separation power of GNNs is equivalent to that of the WL test.
Similar results can be found in previous literature, see e.g.
\[[xu2019powerful]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
[azizian2020expressive]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self},
[geerts2022expressiveness]{.ltx_ref .ltx_missing_citation
.ltx_ref_self}\]. We first introduce some lemmas that can directly imply
Theorem [[4.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S4.Thmtheorem2 "Theorem 4.2. ‣ Separation power ‣ 4. Sketch of proof ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}.
The lemma below, similar to \[[xu2019powerful]{.ltx_ref
.ltx_missing_citation .ltx_ref_self}\]\*Lemma 2, states that the
separation power of GNNs is at most that of the WL test.
:::

::: {#A3.Thmtheorem1 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_lemma}
###### [[Lemma C.1]{#A3.Thmtheorem1.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A3.Thmtheorem1.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#lemma-c.1. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A3.Thmtheorem1.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$.
If ${(G,H)} \sim {(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$, then for any
$F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{𝐺𝑁𝑁}}^{W}$, there exists a permutation
$\sigma_{W} \in S_{n}$ such that
${F_{W}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\sigma_{W}\operatorname{}{({F_{W}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}})}}$.]{#A3.Thmtheorem1.p1.5.5
.ltx_text .ltx_font_italic}
:::
:::

::: {#A3.4 .ltx_proof}
###### Proof of Lemma [[C.1]{.ltx_text .ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A3.Thmtheorem1 "Lemma C.1. ‣ Appendix C Separation power of graph neural networks ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}. {#proof-of-lemma-c.1. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_font_italic .ltx_title_proof}

::: {#A3.1.p1 .ltx_para}
First we describe the sketch of our proof. The assumption
${(G,H)} \sim {(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ implies that, if we apply the WL test
on $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$, the test results should be exactly
the same whatever the hash functions in the WL test we choose. In the
first step, we define a set of hash functions that are injective on all
possible inputs. Second, we show that, if we apply an arbitrarily chosen
GNN: $F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}^{W}$ on $(G,H)$ and
$(\hat{G},\hat{H})$, the vertex features of the two graphs are exactly
the same up to permutation, given the fact that the WL test results are
the same. Finally, it concludes that $F_{W}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}$
should be the same with $F_{W}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ up to
permutation.
:::

::: {#A3.2.p2 .ltx_para}
Let us first define hash functions. We choose $\text{HASH}_{0,V}$ and
$\text{HASH}_{0,W}$ that are injective on the following sets (not
multisets) respectively:

  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\{ h_{1}^{V},\ldots,h_{m}^{V},{\hat{h}}_{1}^{V},\ldots,{\hat{h}}_{m}^{V}\}}\operatorname{}\text{and}\operatorname{}{\{ h_{1}^{W},\ldots,h_{n}^{W},{\hat{h}}_{1}^{W},\ldots,{\hat{h}}_{n}^{W}\}}}.$$   
  -- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

Let
${\{ C_{i}^{{l - 1},V}\}}_{i = 1}^{m},{\{ C_{j}^{{l - 1},W}\}}_{j = 1}^{n}$
and
${\{{\hat{C}}_{i}^{{l - 1},V}\}}_{i = 1}^{m},{\{{\hat{C}}_{j}^{{l - 1},W}\}}_{j = 1}^{n}$
be the vertex colors in the $({l - 1})$-th iteration
$({1 \leq l \leq L})$ in the WL test for $(G,H)$ and $(\hat{G},\hat{H})$
respectively. Define two sets (not multisets) that collect different
colors:

  -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${\mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{V} = {\{ C_{1}^{{l - 1},V},\ldots,C_{m}^{{l - 1},V},{\hat{C}}_{1}^{{l - 1},V},\ldots,{\hat{C}}_{m}^{{l - 1},V}\}}},$$   
  -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

and

  -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${\mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{W} = {\{ C_{1}^{{l - 1},W},\ldots,C_{n}^{{l - 1},W},{\hat{C}}_{1}^{{l - 1},W},\ldots,{\hat{C}}_{n}^{{l - 1},W}\}}}.$$   
  -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

The hash function $\text{HASH}_{l,V}^{\prime}$ and
$\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}$ are chosen such that the outputs are
located in some linear spaces and that
$\{{\text{HASH}_{l,V}^{\prime}\operatorname{}{(C)}}:{C \in \mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{V}}\}$
and
$\{{\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}{(C)}}:{C \in \mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{W}}\}$
are both linearly independent. Finally, we choose hash functions
$\text{HASH}_{l,V}$ and $\text{HASH}_{l,W}$ such that
$\text{HASH}_{l,V}$ is injective on the set (not multiset)

  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $$\begin{matrix}                                                                                                                                                                                                           
     \left\{ \left( C_{i}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} \right)}} \right):{1 \leq i \leq m} \right\} \\                          
     {{\cup \left\{ \left( {\hat{C}}_{i}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( {\hat{C}}_{j}^{{l - 1},W} \right)}} \right):{1 \leq i \leq m} \right\}},}   
     \end{matrix}$$                                                                                                                                                                                                             
  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

and that $\text{HASH}_{l,W}$ is injective on the set (not multiset)

  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $$\begin{matrix}                                                                                                                                                                                                           
     \left\{ \left( C_{j}^{{l - 1},W},{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,V}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( C_{i}^{{l - 1},V} \right)}} \right):{1 \leq j \leq n} \right\} \\                          
     {{\cup \left\{ \left( {\hat{C}}_{j}^{{l - 1},W},{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,V}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( {\hat{C}}_{i}^{{l - 1},V} \right)}} \right):{1 \leq j \leq n} \right\}}.}   
     \end{matrix}$$                                                                                                                                                                                                             
  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

Those hash functions give the vertex colors at the next iteration
($l$-th layer): ${\{ C_{i}^{l,V}\}}_{i = 1}^{m}$,
${\{ C_{j}^{l,W}\}}_{j = 1}^{n}$ and
${\{{\hat{C}}_{i}^{l,V}\}}_{i = 1}^{m}$,
${\{{\hat{C}}_{j}^{l,W}\}}_{j = 1}^{n}$.
:::

::: {#A3.3.p3 .ltx_para}
Consider any $F_{W} \in \mathcal{F}_{\text{GNN}}^{W}$ and let
${\{ h_{i}^{{l - 1},V}\}}_{i = 1}^{m},{\{ h_{j}^{{l - 1},W}\}}_{j = 1}^{n}$
and
${\{{\hat{h}}_{i}^{{l - 1},V}\}}_{i = 1}^{m},{\{{\hat{h}}_{j}^{{l - 1},W}\}}_{j = 1}^{n}$
be the vertex features in the $l$-th layer ($0 \leq l \leq L$) of the
graph neural network $F_{W}$. (Update rule refers to equations
([[2.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E1 "2.1 ‣ 2.2. Graph neural networks for LP ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),([[2.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E2 "2.2 ‣ 2.2. Graph neural networks for LP ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),([[2.3]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E3 "2.3 ‣ 2.2. Graph neural networks for LP ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}),([[2.5]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#S2.E5 "2.5 ‣ 2.2. Graph neural networks for LP ‣ 2. Preliminaries ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref}))
We aim to prove by induction that for any $l \in {\{ 0,1,\ldots,L\}}$,
the followings hold:

-   [[(i)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A3.I1.ix1}

    ::: {#A3.I1.ix1.p1 .ltx_para}
    $C_{i}^{l,V} = C_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}$ implies
    $h_{i}^{l,V} = h_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}$, for
    ${1 \leq i},{i^{\prime} \leq m}$;
    :::
-   [[(ii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A3.I1.ix2}

    ::: {#A3.I1.ix2.p1 .ltx_para}
    ${\hat{C}}_{i}^{l,V} = {\hat{C}}_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}$ implies
    ${\hat{h}}_{i}^{l,V} = {\hat{h}}_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}$, for
    ${1 \leq i},{i^{\prime} \leq m}$;
    :::
-   [[(iii)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A3.I1.ix3}

    ::: {#A3.I1.ix3.p1 .ltx_para}
    $C_{i}^{l,V} = {\hat{C}}_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}$ implies
    $h_{i}^{l,V} = {\hat{h}}_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}$, for
    ${1 \leq i},{i^{\prime} \leq m}$;
    :::
-   [[(iv)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A3.I1.ix4}

    ::: {#A3.I1.ix4.p1 .ltx_para}
    $C_{j}^{l,W} = C_{j^{\prime}}^{l,W}$ implies
    $h_{j}^{l,W} = h_{j^{\prime}}^{l,W}$, for
    ${1 \leq j},{j^{\prime} \leq n}$;
    :::
-   [[(v)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A3.I1.ix5}

    ::: {#A3.I1.ix5.p1 .ltx_para}
    ${\hat{C}}_{j}^{l,W} = {\hat{C}}_{j^{\prime}}^{l,W}$ implies
    ${\hat{h}}_{j}^{l,W} = {\hat{h}}_{j^{\prime}}^{l,W}$, for
    ${1 \leq j},{j^{\prime} \leq n}$;
    :::
-   [[(vi)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_item}]{#A3.I1.ix6}

    ::: {#A3.I1.ix6.p1 .ltx_para}
    $C_{j}^{l,W} = {\hat{C}}_{j^{\prime}}^{l,W}$ implies
    $h_{j}^{l,W} = {\hat{h}}_{j^{\prime}}^{l,W}$, for
    ${1 \leq j},{j^{\prime} \leq n}$.
    :::

The above claims (i)-(vi) are clearly true for $l = 0$ due to the
injectivity of $\text{HASH}_{0,V}$ and $\text{HASH}_{0,W}$. Now we
assume that (i)-(vi) are true for some
${l - 1} \in {\{ 0,1,\ldots,{L - 1}\}}$. Suppose that
$C_{i}^{l,V} = C_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}$, i.e.,

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --
     $$\begin{matrix}                                                                                                                                                                                                         
     {\text{HASH}_{l,V}\operatorname{}\left( C_{i}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} \right)}} \right)} \\                         
     {{= {\text{HASH}_{l,V}\operatorname{}\left( C_{i^{\prime}}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i^{\prime},j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} \right)}} \right)}},}   
     \end{matrix}$$                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --

for some ${1 \leq i},{i^{\prime} \leq m}$. It follows from the
injectivity of $\text{HASH}_{l,V}$ that

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(C.1)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${C_{i}^{{l - 1},V} = C_{i^{\prime}}^{{l - 1},V}},$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- ------------------------------------------------------- --

and

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} \right)}} = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i^{\prime},j}\operatorname{}\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}\operatorname{}\left( C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} \right)}}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

According to the linearly independent property of
$\text{HASH}_{l,W}^{\prime}$, the above equation implies that

  ----------------------------------------------------- -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
  [(C.2)]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_equation .ltx_align_left}      $${{{\sum\limits_{C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C}E_{i,j}} = {\sum\limits_{C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C}E_{i^{\prime},j}}},{{\forall C} \in \mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{W}}}.$$   
  ----------------------------------------------------- -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

Note that the induction assumption guarantees that
$h_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = h_{j^{\prime}}^{{l - 1},W}$ as long as
$C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C_{j^{\prime}}^{{l - 1},W}$. So one can assign for
each $C \in \mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{W}$ some
${h\operatorname{}{(C)}} \in {\mathbb{R}}^{d_{l - 1}}$ such that
$h_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = {h\operatorname{}{(C)}}$ as long as
$C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C$ for any $1 \leq j \leq n$. Therefore, it follows
from ([[C.2]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A3.E2 "C.2 ‣ Proof of Lemma C.1. ‣ Appendix C Separation power of graph neural networks ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
that

  -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     ${\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}{(h_{j}^{{l - 1},W})}}} =$   $\sum\limits_{C \in \mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{W}}{\sum\limits_{C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}{({h\operatorname{}{(C)}})}}}$                                                                                                                           
     $=$                                                                                                  ${{\sum\limits_{C \in \mathbf{C}_{l - 1}^{W}}{\sum\limits_{C_{j}^{{l - 1},W} = C}{E_{i^{\prime},j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}{({h\operatorname{}{(C)}})}}}} = {\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i^{\prime},j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}{(h_{j}^{{l - 1},W})}}}}.$   
  -- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

Note also that ([[C.1]{.ltx_text
.ltx_ref_tag}](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2209.12288#A3.E1 "C.1 ‣ Proof of Lemma C.1. ‣ Appendix C Separation power of graph neural networks ‣ On Representing Linear Programs by Graph Neural Networks"){.ltx_ref})
and the induction assumption lead to
$h_{i}^{{l - 1},V} = h_{i^{\prime}}^{{l - 1},V}$. Then one can conclude
that

  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${h_{i}^{l,V} = {g_{l}^{V}\operatorname{}\left( h_{i}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i,j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}{(h_{j}^{{l - 1},W})}}} \right)} = {g_{l}^{V}\operatorname{}\left( h_{i^{\prime}}^{{l - 1},V},{\sum\limits_{j = 1}^{n}{E_{i^{\prime},j}\operatorname{}f_{l}^{W}\operatorname{}{(h_{j}^{{l - 1},W})}}} \right)} = h_{i^{\prime}}^{l,V}}.$$   
  -- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

This proves the claim (i) for $l$. The other five claims can be proved
using similar arguments.
:::

::: {#A3.4.p4 .ltx_para}
Therefore, we obtain from ${(G,H)} \sim {(\hat{G},\hat{H})}$ that

  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${\left\{ \left\{ h_{1}^{L,V},h_{2}^{L,V},\ldots,h_{m}^{L,V} \right\} \right\} = \left\{ \left\{ {\hat{h}}_{1}^{L,V},{\hat{h}}_{2}^{L,V},\ldots,{\hat{h}}_{m}^{L,V} \right\} \right\}},$$   
  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

and that

  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --
     $${\left\{ \left\{ h_{1}^{L,W},h_{2}^{L,W},\ldots,h_{n}^{L,W} \right\} \right\} = \left\{ \left\{ {\hat{h}}_{1}^{L,W},{\hat{h}}_{2}^{L,W},\ldots,{\hat{h}}_{n}^{L,W} \right\} \right\}}.$$   
  -- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --

By the definition of the output layer, the above conclusion guarantees
that
${F_{W}\operatorname{}{(G,H)}} = {\sigma_{W}\operatorname{}{({F_{W}\operatorname{}{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}})}}$
for some $\sigma_{W} \in S_{n}$. ∎
:::
:::

::: {#A3.Thmtheorem2 .ltx_theorem .ltx_theorem_lemma}
###### [[Lemma C.2]{#A3.Thmtheorem2.1.1.1 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold}]{.ltx_tag .ltx_tag_theorem}[.]{#A3.Thmtheorem2.2.2 .ltx_text .ltx_font_bold} {#lemma-c.2. .ltx_title .ltx_runin .ltx_title_theorem}

::: {#A3.Thmtheorem2.p1 .ltx_para}
[Let
${{(G,H)},{(\hat{G},\hat{H})}} \in {\mathcal{G}_{m,n} \times \mathcal{H}_{m}^{V} \times \mathcal{H}_{n}^{W}}$.
